system.description:
The Oni Hunter exists because Tesshari's enhancement technology produces failures that the Caste hierarchy refuses to acknowledge officially. When an individual's Echomind — the resonant soul-structure that sits in the space between flesh and metal — becomes misaligned from its enhancement network, the process of dissolution is not quiet. There are stages. In the early stages, the person loses refinement: their modifications function but their judgment degrades, their language becomes imprecise, their personality flattens and then sharpens into something colder. In the middle stages, they are what the Sutensai call Resonance-Broken: present in body but absent in the specifically human way, capable only of the most basic drives. In the terminal stage — the full Shell-state — the Echomind has departed entirely. What remains is an enhancement network with a meat driver, an automaton that retains the combat capabilities of whoever it used to be without any of the judgment, mercy, or restraint that prevented those capabilities from being fully deployed against everything in reach.

The Iron Afterlife produces a different but related category of problem. The digital realm where the dead reside is not static — it is a vast, slowly evolving ecosystem of preserved Echominds, and not all of those Echominds accepted their death quietly. Some find ways to push back through the Wire network. Some are pulled through by people who should know better, performing rituals that are not quite Sutensai practice and not quite Wire hacking and are definitely dangerous. And occasionally — rarely, but often enough to require a professional response — something that was never human finds its way into the resonant frequency of the physical world and operates there with the malice of the aggrieved dead and the incomprehensible persistence of something that has no body to protect.

The Oni Hunter is the professional answer to all of this. They did not exist as a formal tradition before the Caste hierarchy's enhancement program reached sufficient scale for the failures to become common — which means they are a relatively new category in Tesshari's martial culture, without the formal codes and institutional structures of the samurai orders. What they have instead is a body of working knowledge: what the stages of Echomind dissolution look like, how Shell-states move and fight and can be disrupted, what Iron Afterlife entities need to manifest physically and how to deny them those needs. They have the tools, the techniques, and the specific psychological architecture that allows a person to hunt other people — because that is what the job is. Not monsters, technically. People, at the point where personhood has failed in specific, catastrophic, and terrifying ways.

The cultural position of the Oni Hunter is liminal in a way that is difficult to navigate. They are necessary and they are avoided. People hire them and do not invite them to dinner. They know things about the Echomind's failure states that most people prefer not to know. They have, in many cases, killed people who were recognizable to their families as still-present — families who watched the Oni Hunter kill someone who still looked like their mother, their child, their friend, even as the Oni Hunter explained that what was left was not, in any meaningful sense, that person anymore. This is not a comfortable thing to be. It is also not a comfortable thing to stop being, once you know what you know.
